CONCLUSION AND FUTURE WORK In this paper, we demonstrate how to train a log-based anomaly detection model that can prioritise the prediction of higher severity anomalies on drone flight logs while increasing the prediction confidence score at the same time. Considering the nature of the dataset, where samples that belong to different severity levels share common features, a multitask vector label representation along with severity-oriented decoding is proposed. user_input: I. INTRODUCTION I. INTRODUCTION The use of drones has become increasingly common in various applications, including aerial photography, surveying, and monitoring. However, drones can also be involved in incidents such as accidents, violations, or even crimes, making it necessary to investigate drone logs as part of forensic investigations. In a forensic investigation, drone logs are essential sources of information that can help investigators reconstruct the events leading up to an incident. Drone logs typically include information such as flight paths, altitude, speed, battery life, sensor data, and other telemetry information . reference: I. INTRODUCTION I. INTRODUCTION The use of drones has become increasingly common in various applications, including aerial photography, surveying, and monitoring. However, drones can also be involved in incidents such as accidents, violations, or even crimes, making it necessary to investigate drone logs as part of forensic investigations. In a forensic investigation, drone logs are essential sources of information that can help investigators reconstruct the events leading up to an incident. Drone logs typically include information such as flight paths, altitude, speed, battery life, sensor data, and other telemetry information . Important analysis of drone logs involves the identification of the most critical pieces of information in the log data and the analysis of how they relate to the incident

Point anomaly detection characterises each log entry, whether an event is an abnormality or a normal event. For instance, if a user types the wrong password when trying to gain access to an operating system prompt, the generated log message can be considered as an anomaly , , . Considering the nature of the dataset, where samples that belong to different severity levels share common features, a multitask vector label representation along with severity-oriented decoding is proposed. In a binary setting, let the Normal class be positive and the Anomaly is negative. Then, False Positive (FP) refers to samples with a true label negative but predicted as positive. At the same time, a False Negative (FN) is a case when a sample belongs to a positive class but is predicted as negative. However, in this study, we define the misclassification cases differently: • False Positive refers to a case when the true class is higher than the predicted class. • False Negative is a misclassification case where the true class is lower than the predicted

user_input: align with the anomaly severity context, where the distance between true and predicted labels matters. If the predicted label is lower than the true label, we define it False Positive (FP). Conversely, if the true label is lower than the predicted label, we define it False Negative (FN). In this context, having less FP is more important than having less FN, as the task objective is anomaly severity detection, where we aim to detect all abnormalities along with the severity level. Therefore, undetected abnormalities, or detecting higher severity as lower ones, indicate that the model fails to meet the task’s objectives. reference: DATASET The DEAP dataset has 32 participants with a composition of 50% male and 50% female samples . The stimulus used is a video with a duration of 60 seconds. The results of the EEG recording have a duration of 63 seconds, of which 3 seconds are the baseline. One participant has 40 recordings of the EEG trials. The results of the EEG trial recordings have been mea- sured using the Self-Assessment Manikin (SAM). SAM has four emotional dimensions, namely valence, arousal, domi- nance, and liking, with ratings 0-9. For valence and arousal classes, emotion labels are divided into two categories: low and high. Models pretrained on these datasets have optimized weights for recognizing common features, mitigating issues associated with small datasets. Transfer learning and fine-tuning allow models to adapt to new datasets, significantly reducing training time. Studies utilizing pretrained models include for detect- ing polyps with YOLOv4, for Crohn’s disease lesions, for pre-colorectal cancer abnormalities, for bladder cancer lesions, and for cervical lesions. CONCLUSION This research demonstrates that the YOLOv8 model, specifically the YOLOv8m variant, provides excellent results in detecting Peritoneal Carcinomatosis (PC) from the used laparoscopic image dataset. By utilizing and fine-tuning the pre-trained YOLOv8 weights, this model shows superior performance with a mean Average Precision (mAP50) of 0.871, precision of 0.893, recall of 0.785, and an F1 score of 0.84. The predicted images also show that the performance of YOLOv8 with pre-trained weights is able to predict images well. All YOLOv8 scales are accurate in making predictions, with no errors in image prediction. In contrast, without using pre-trained weights, YOLOv8n and YOLOv8s still make errors in prediction, with some lesions being missed.
